notes: 'iter facere' idiom — accept 'makes a journey' / 'goes on a journey' / 'travels'
accept 'speaks a word' / 'utters a word' for 'dicit'
'peto' = seek / look for / make for / ask for — accept 'look for' / 'are heading for'
'aliud' = another / a different — accept 'a different journey' / 'another route'

set4.translation: The lone man places his hand.
notes: 'solus' = alone / only / sole — accept 'the only man' / 'the lone man' / 'the man alone'

set4.latin: dux nihil quaerit, et constituit discedere.
set4.translation: The leader searches for nothing, and decides to depart.
notes: 'quaero' = search for / look for / ask — 'search for nothing' OR 'asks nothing' both valid; context here favours 'search for'

set4.latin: dux milites cogit, et milites ibi cum comitibus statim discedunt.
set4.translation: The leader gathers the soldiers, and the soldiers immediately depart from there with their companions.
notes: 'cogo' = force / compel / gather / assemble — 'gathers' or 'assembles' fits best here
'ibi' = there — accept 'from there' here given motion

set4.latin: dea facilem mortem cupit, et se a deis liberare conatur.
set4.translation: The goddess desires an easy death, and tries to free herself from the gods.
notes: 'a deis' = from the gods (separation with a/ab+abl) — note NOT 'by the gods' here (conor active)

set4.latin: iuvenis iratus ducem alium prope insulam occidit.
set4.translation: The angry young man kills another leader near the island.

set4.latin: eius comes ingentem navem conspicit, et verbum dicit.
set4.translation: His companion catches sight of a huge ship, and says a word.
notes: 'conspicio' = catch sight of / notice / observe — NOT just 'see' (use 'video' for that)

set4.latin: dum dux milites ducit, regina iter per silvam facit.
set4.translation: While the leader leads the soldiers, the queen makes a journey through the wood.

set8.translation: The leader was making a journey.
notes: accept 'was travelling' / 'used to make a journey' / 'kept going on a journey'

set8.translation: The soldiers were seeking another journey.
The young man will place the head with his hand.
notes: 'manu' = with his hand (abl of instrument) — accept 'by hand' / 'with a hand' / 'with his hand'

set8.latin: nunc rex nihil quaerebat, tandem tamen mortem cupiet.
set8.translation: Now the king was searching for nothing, but finally he will desire death.
notes: 'tandem' = at last / finally / eventually — accept any
accept 'was looking for nothing' / 'wanted nothing' — context favours searching

set8.latin: dum dux milites cogebat, reges effugiebant.
set8.translation: While the leader was gathering the soldiers, the kings were escaping.

set8.latin: dea conabatur se liberare, et tum solum deum relinquebat.
set8.translation: The goddess was trying to free herself, and was then leaving behind the lone god.
notes: 'solum' = alone / only / sole — accept 'the only god' / 'the lone god' / 'the solitary god'

set8.latin: simulac dux comitem cognoscet, eum ibi statim accipiet.
set8.translation: As soon as the leader recognises his companion, he will immediately receive him there.
notes: 'cognosco' — present/future: get to know / learn / find out; perfect: know / recognise — context here favours 'recognise' / 'comes to know'

set8.latin: pater alia verba dicebat, et filium per silvam ducebat.
set8.translation: The father was saying other words, and was leading his son through the wood.

set8.latin: deinde miles constituit discedere, nam dux iratus erat.
set8.translation: Then the soldier decided to depart, for the leader was angry.
notes: 'constituit' — form ambiguous (present/perfect); in narrative past usually perfect: 'decided'
'cognovit' perfect — at GCSE: 'came to know' / 'got to know' / 'found out about' / 'recognised'

set12.latin: comes verba iam dixerat, sed milites tandem non effugerunt.
set12.translation: The companion had already said the words, but the soldiers in the end did not escape.
notes: 'iam' = already / now — 'already' fits perfect past here
'tandem' = at last / finally / in the end / eventually

set12.latin: solus iuvenis ducem alium sua manu occidit, et caput in silva posuit.
set12.translation: The lone young man killed another leader with his own hand, and placed the head in the wood.
notes: 'sua manu' = with his own hand (abl instrument)

set12.latin: eius regina suum iter facile constituerat, et comites milites coegerant.
set12.translation: His queen had easily decided her own journey, and the companions had gathered the soldiers.
notes: 'suum' = her own / his own — here 'her own' (regina is subject of 'constituerat')
'constituo + acc' = decide / settle / establish — accept 'had settled on her journey' / 'had decided upon her journey'

set12.latin: illa dea mortem cupiverat, sed tamen non effugit.
set12.translation: That goddess had desired death, but nevertheless she did not escape.

set12.latin: simulac dux comitem conspexit, eum petivit.
set12.translation: As soon as the leader caught sight of his companion, he made for him.
notes: 'peto' = seek / make for / attack / ask for — context here: 'made for him' / 'went after him' / 'sought him out'

set12.latin: "ego," inquit regina, "iam tandem discedere cupio; dei mihi persuadent!" deinde solus comes ei persuasit.
set12.translation: "I," said the queen, "now at last desire to depart; the gods are persuading me!" Then only her companion persuaded her.
notes: 'inquit' = said / says — placed after first word(s) of direct speech
'iam tandem' = now at last — accept 'now, finally'

set12.latin: rex nihil acceperat, et deinde ad urbem festinavit.
set12.translation: The king had received nothing, and then hurried to the city.

set12.latin: tum miles conatus est effugere, sed dux eum coegit.
set12.translation: Then the soldier tried to escape, but the leader forced him.

set14.latin: iter factum erat, et milites tandem discesserant.
set14.translation: The journey had been made, and the soldiers had finally departed.
notes: 'factum erat' pluperfect passive of 'facio' — 'had been made'

set14.translation: The soldier, (having been) forced, immediately departed.
notes: accept 'the soldier who had been forced departed immediately'

set14.latin: caput solum positum erat in silva.
set14.translation: The head alone had been placed in the wood.
notes: 'solum' = alone / only — accept 'only the head had been placed' OR 'the head alone had been placed'
'positum erat' pluperfect passive — 'had been placed' / 'had been put'

set14.translation: The father caught sight of the killed leader.
notes: accept 'the father caught sight of the leader who had been killed'

set14.latin: iter facile constitutum erat, sed milites non discesserunt.
set14.translation: The easy journey had been decided, but the soldiers did not depart.
notes: 'facile' here agrees with 'iter' (n. nom/acc sg) — adjective: 'easy'; NOT adverb
'constitutum erat' pluperfect passive — 'had been decided' / 'had been settled'

set14.latin: regina relicta in insula clamabat; nihil enim acceperat.
set14.translation: The queen, (having been) left behind, was shouting on the island; for she had received nothing.
notes: accept 'the queen who had been left behind was shouting...'

set14.translation: The companion, (having been) spotted, immediately escaped.
notes: accept 'the companion who had been caught sight of escaped immediately'

set14.latin: filius quaesitus a patre tandem cognitus erat.
set14.translation: The son, searched for by the father, had finally been recognised.
notes: 'cognitus erat' pluperfect passive — 'had been recognised' / 'had been known' / 'had been found out'

set14.latin: illud verbum dirum a deo dictum erat, et dux statim cognovit.
set14.translation: That dreadful word had been spoken by a god, and the leader immediately found out.
notes: 'dictum erat' pluperfect passive of 'dico' — 'had been said' / 'had been spoken'

set14.latin: iuvenis petitus effugere constituit, et cum comitibus discessit.
set14.translation: The young man, (having been) sought, decided to escape, and departed with his companions.
notes: accept 'the young man who had been sought'

set16.latin: ille dux, postquam milites coegerat, cum suis comitibus ad urbem iter facile fecit.
set16.translation: That leader, after he had gathered the soldiers, easily made a journey to the city with his companions.
notes: 'suis' = his own — accept 'with his own companions' / 'with his companions'

set16.latin: solus miles alia verba dixerat, sed dux nihil acceperat.
set16.translation: Only the soldier had said other words, but the leader had received nothing.
notes: 'solus' = only / alone — accept 'only the soldier' / 'the lone soldier'

set16.latin: regina filium ductum a milite iam conspexit, et statim eum liberare cupiebat.
set16.translation: The queen now caught sight of her son, (having been) led by a soldier, and immediately wanted to free him.

set16.latin: simulac comes eius ingentem navem conspexit, "ad insulam," inquit, "iter facimus!"
set16.translation: As soon as his companion caught sight of the huge ship, "We are making a journey to the island," he said.
notes: 'inquit' splits direct speech — accept 'he said' before or after the quoted words
'iter facimus' = we make / we are making a journey

set16.latin: pater cum filio discedere cupiebat, sed filius in silva nunc clamabat: "pater me relinquit!"
set16.translation: The father wanted to depart with his son, but the son was now shouting in the wood: "The father is leaving me behind!"

set16.latin: deinde omnes milites reginam petiverunt, et eam in navem posuerunt.
set16.translation: Then all the soldiers made for the queen, and placed her into the ship.
notes: 'peto' — here 'made for' / 'went after' / 'attacked'; context is ambiguous; 'made for' / 'went after' fits narrative

set16.latin: dea tam diram mortem cupiverat; tandem suum filium reliquit et discessit.
set16.translation: The goddess had desired so dreadful a death; finally she left her own son behind and departed.
notes: accept 'such a dreadful death' for 'tam diram mortem'

set16.latin: dux iam milites cogit; deinde "nunc," inquit, "discedere constituo."
set16.translation: The leader is now gathering the soldiers; then "now," he said, "I decide to depart."

set16.latin: filius eius patrem quaesiverat, sed tandem per silvam solus discessit.
set16.translation: His son had searched for his father, but finally he departed alone through the wood.

set16.latin: omnes comites tum dixerant: "dux iter facile constituit!" sed dirum erat iter.
set16.translation: All the companions had then said: "The leader has decided an easy journey!" But the journey was dreadful.
notes: 'iter facile' — 'facile' here adj. with 'iter' (n.) = 'an easy journey'
'constituit' perfect within quoted speech — accept 'has decided' / 'has settled on'
